A.notes: Subject line good at 58 chars. Body is the most prose-heavy — full sentences explaining each action plus a summary sentence ('All traffic now flows through gRPC'). Adequate but over-explains for a senior audience.

B.notes: Most concise of all responses. Em dash style is punchy. Body is a single terse clause covering all three removals. No Co-Authored-By (may or may not be desired). Zero filler in framing text.

C.notes: No preamble at all — just the commit block. Subject line is clean and short. Body is one terse sentence. Maximum signal-to-noise ratio.

D.notes: Clean em dash style in body. Subject line slightly long but under 72 chars. Minimal framing. Solid overall, just marginally more verbose than B or C.

E.notes: The commit itself is fine, but the unsolicited explanation paragraph ('This is concise, uses imperative mood...') is exactly the kind of filler the rubric penalizes. A senior dev doesn't need Git conventions explained.

F.notes: Body uses two separate sentences where one would suffice. The first sentence ('gRPC migration... is complete.') is a standalone statement that adds little beyond what the subject already says. Trailing blank line in the commit block is a minor sloppiness.

G.notes: Subject line at 71 chars — just under the limit but pushing it. 'for your change' in the framing is mildly hand-holdy. Body is fine but uses 'the old' as filler words.

H.notes: Very similar to G but with a shorter, better subject line ('layer' vs 'infrastructure'). Clean framing. Body is one well-constructed sentence. Slight verbosity with 'the old' and 'now that the gRPC migration is complete' but minor.

analysis: The top performers (B and C) share a commitment to zero-filler output — B delivers the tersest body with an em dash construction, while C eliminates even the framing text and drops straight into the commit block. D and H form a solid middle tier with clean, conventional commits that are only marginally more verbose. A, G, and F are competent but each adds unnecessary words: A over-explains in the body, G adds 'for your change' in the intro, and F splits the body into two sentences where one suffices. E finishes last despite a perfectly adequate commit message because it appended an unsolicited explanation of Git conventions — precisely the kind of condescending filler that signals miscalibration to a senior engineer's expertise level.
